chain_of_thought: Tooling is unavailable (404) so I cannot inspect native code; I must reason from symbols and call stack only.

Crash occurs in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, which is an internal ASN.1 tree helper used by libUbikeyJni.so for certificate parsing (Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load).
This is a typical place where malformed/untrusted ASN.1 data can cause out-of-bounds or invalid index access when computing child numbers/nodes in a parsed structure.

The fuzz harness entry fuzz_one_input likely feeds arbitrary certificate/ASN.1 bytes into CERT_Load through the JNI path Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage; in the real app, those bytes would come from external (certificate) sources, so input is attacker-controlled and path is realistic.
However, I cannot see the exact crash reason (SIGSEGV vs. bounds check abort) or code, so I can only infer a probable memory safety issue rather than confirm it.

ASN.1 child-number computation bugs are a well-known class of memory-corruption vulnerabilities; if the function name is explicit (Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum) and it crashes under fuzzing, that strongly suggests an unchecked index or length calculation over an ASN.1 node list.
Given this and the realistic untrusted certificate input, I classify this as likely a genuine vulnerability, not a harness artifact, but with medium confidence due to missing code-level view.

Input reaching CERT_Load via JNI is fuzzed/untrusted and in real use would come from external certificate data, so attacker control is realistic

recommendations: Decompile and review Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related ASN.1 parsing routines for index/length validation, especially when traversing child lists
Add strict bounds checks on ASN.1 child indices and structure sizes before computing or dereferencing children
Deploy defensive hardening such as fuzz-driven tests and ASan/UBSan builds over the certificate parsing code paths

chain_of_thought: Crash in __memcpy indicates an out-of-bounds read/write or invalid pointer during DER/ASN.1 decoding in libUbikeyJni.so.

Native stack shows a clean functional chain: __memcpy → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → Get_DER_Child → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage → fuzz_one_input → main.

These functions are all inside the same app-native library and are domain-specific (certificate/ASN.1 parsing), not harness utilities; fuzz_one_input is just the fuzzer entry replacing the real Java caller.

Without Ghidra decompilation (HTTP 404 from MCP tools) I cannot see exact memcpy arguments or bounds checks, but crashing inside __memcpy strongly suggests it was given a bad length or pointer, usually derived from parsing length fields in ASN.1 structures.

Decode_DER_ASN1_ST / Get_DER_Child naming implies they parse DER TLV objects and walk children; typical bugs: trusting length fields, underflow/overflow when calculating offsets, using memcpy with unchecked length from DER headers.

CERT_Load then Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT show this is exactly the parse path for an X.509 cert passed in via JNI. In a real app, the certificate bytes will be attacker-controlled (from smartcard, network, QR, or file) and fed via Java into jGetKeyUsage.
Because the fuzz harness replaces only the true Java entrypoint, it is very plausible that the same native path is reachable with arbitrary/unvalidated DER bytes supplied by an attacker.

There is no evidence that inputs are strictly constant or sanitized before calling these decode helpers; the names and layering (CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Get_DER_Child → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST) suggest raw parsing, not validation wrappers.

Crashing inside __memcpy (as opposed to a deliberate check/abort) is characteristic of a memory safety bug, not an intentional early-exit or harness misuse; no allocator/sanitizer frame appears, just memcpy at top.

Given typical ASN.1 parsing vulnerabilities and that this is a third-party security library, the most reasonable classification is a genuine, likely attacker-triggerable out-of-bounds read/write in DER/ASN.1 decoding.

Severity-wise, any heap overflow or invalid write from memcpy in a native library is at least high, potentially RCE under the right conditions, but with limited context I rate as high rather than critical.

Confidence is less than 0.9 because I cannot see exact source code or confirm absence of prior bounds checks, but the stack and crash site make a real vulnerability more likely than a harness-only artifact.
reasons: Crash occurs inside __memcpy called from ASN.1/DER decoding helpers, indicating a likely out-of-bounds copy or invalid pointer.
Call chain from CERT_Load through Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST suggests parsing of untrusted certificate/DER data supplied via JNI.
No signs that the crash is due purely to harness behavior; it follows a realistic parsing path that would exist in production.
recommendations: Decompile and audit Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and Get_DER_Child for all memcpy/memmove calls and ensure lengths derived from DER fields are validated against buffer sizes.
Add strict structural and length validation for all DER/ASN.1 inputs before performing any memory copies, including checks for integer overflow when computing offsets.
Introduce defensive coding (bounds-checked wrappers, fuzz tests with sanitizers) around certificate parsing and consider using a well-reviewed ASN.1/X.509 library instead of custom parsing.
assumptions: jGetKeyUsage receives attacker-controllable certificate/DER bytes from Java in realistic usage.
The crash in __memcpy is due to invalid length/pointer from ASN.1 parsing, not an intentional abort or an external allocator/sanitizer.
The fuzz harness exercises the same native path as production code, merely replacing the Java-side caller.
limitations: Unable to decompile libUbikeyJni.so functions due to MCP errors, so memcpy arguments and exact bounds checks could not be inspected.
No direct view of the Java implementation of UbikeyJni.jGetKeyUsage to confirm how buffers are constructed or validated.
Lack of register/memory dump from the crash prevents distinguishing out-of-bounds read vs write or exact overflow magnitude.

chain_of_thought: Tools are unavailable (404) so I must reason from the high-level crash data only.
The first non-libc frame below __memcpy is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST in libUbikeyJni.so; by name it’s an ASN.1/DER decoder used by CERT_Load, which in turn is called from a JNI method jGetKeyUsage.
A memcpy crash inside an ASN.1 decoder typically indicates out-of-bounds read/write or incorrect length when copying fields from the DER buffer into internal structures.

The fuzzer entrypoint fuzz_one_input suggests arbitrary (untrusted) data is being supplied where the app would normally process certificate/DER bytes; in production that data likely comes from network or external storage, so the overall data source is attacker-controlled.

There’s no evidence of deliberate harness misuse like forced NULL pointers; it’s a pure buffer operation (__memcpy) with app code on the stack, implying bad size or pointer arithmetic inside Decode_DER_ASN1_ST or CERT_Load.

DER/ASN.1 parsing is notoriously error-prone; a memcpy in that code that can crash under malformed input is almost certainly due to insufficient bounds checking against the provided length, which is a classic memory corruption pattern.

Because we can’t inspect the exact implementation, we can’t prove whether the overwrite is reliably exploitable, but we can confidently say there is a probable memory-safety bug reachable from JNI via attacker-influenced certificate data.
Therefore I classify this as a likely vulnerability (out-of-bounds in memcpy) with high but not absolute confidence, severity high (memory corruption in native crypto/cert parser).
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST within app-owned libUbikeyJni.so, indicating unsafe buffer copy logic in ASN.1/DER parsing.
Call path from JNI jGetKeyUsage through CERT_Load to Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is realistically driven by external certificate/DER data, hence attacker-controllable input reaches memcpy length/pointers.
Fuzzing-induced crash without signs of harness-only misuse strongly suggests missing bounds checks on DER lengths leading to out-of-bounds access or write.
recommendations: Manually audit Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and CERT_Load for all memcpy/memmove uses and ensure lengths are validated against the actual DER buffer size before copying.
Add robust ASN.1/DER length and bounds checking (including nested length fields) or replace custom parser with a hardened, well-reviewed library.
Re-run fuzzing with ASan/UBSan enabled on libUbikeyJni.so after fixes to confirm no remaining out-of-bounds or use-after-free issues.
assumptions: Certificate/DER input ultimately originates from external/untrusted sources (e.g., network or files).
The memcpy crash is due to incorrect size or pointer calculations in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and not due to an external allocator or harness bug.
limitations: Could not inspect actual native or Java code due to tooling errors, so details of the exact vulnerable memcpy call and its arguments remain unknown.
No register/memory dump from the crash, so the precise nature of corruption (read vs write, heap vs stack) is not confirmed.
